title: Phase spirals across galactic disks I: Exploring dynamical influences on winding
comment: 19 pages, 10 figures, submitted to ApJ
summary: The vertical phase-space spirals in the Milky Way are clear evidence of disequilibrium. However, they are challenging to study because phase mixing signals evolve under the influence of many different dynamical processes and can be driven by many sources of disequilibrium. We characterize phase spirals in two simulations -- one test particle and one N-body -- with basis function expansions, using these to derive winding times ($T_{\rm fit}$). We find that phase spirals in the test particle simulation wind up as expected from pure phase mixing theory while those in the self-consistent simulation do not. Specifically, in the N-body simulation we find that (i) the onset of winding is delayed, (ii) the winding rate is slowed, and (iii) the rate of winding oscillates with time. The extent of these effects depends on the azimuthal action $J_φ$ of the phase spiral region. We build some physical intuition for these effects through 1-D toy models which follow a group of co-moving stars traveling through several different evolving potentials. We find that phase spiral winding can be delayed until the group no longer moves coherently with the midplane of the (perturbed) potential and oscillates with time as the group experiences (e.g.) a breathing mode traveling through the disk. Rates of winding change as the vertical structure of the disk evolves. The modifications to winding are strongest in the inner galaxy where the disk potential dominates. We conclude that in the Milky Way, all calculations of the winding time should be interpreted as lower limits and that the most trustworthy winding times are likely in the outer disk.

AI.tldr: Phase spirals in Milky Way simulations show different winding behaviors: test particles follow pure phase mixing theory, while self-consistent N-body simulations exhibit delayed onset, slowed rates, and oscillating winding patterns due to evolving disk potentials and coherent star group motions.
AI.motivation: To understand why vertical phase-space spirals in the Milky Way don't behave as predicted by pure phase mixing theory, and to characterize how self-consistent gravitational interactions affect phase spiral winding in realistic galactic simulations.
AI.method: Used basis function expansions to characterize phase spirals in two simulations (test particle and N-body), derived winding times, and developed 1-D toy models to track co-moving star groups through evolving potentials to build physical intuition.
AI.result: Phase spirals in test particle simulations wind up as expected from pure phase mixing theory, but N-body simulations show: (1) delayed winding onset, (2) slowed winding rates, (3) oscillating winding rates over time. Effects are strongest in inner galaxy where disk potential dominates and depend on azimuthal action Jφ.
AI.conclusion: In the Milky Way, all winding time calculations should be interpreted as lower limits; the most trustworthy winding times are likely in the outer disk where self-gravity effects are weaker. Phase spiral evolution is significantly modified by self-consistent gravitational interactions and evolving disk structure.

title: oMEGACat. X. Shedding light on the disrupted dwarf galaxy of Omega Centauri
comment: 25 pages, 17 figures, 1 table, and 1 appendix. Submitted to ApJ
summary: Omega Centauri ($ω\,$Cen) is the most massive and chemically complex star cluster in the Milky Way and is widely regarded as the surviving nuclear star cluster of an accreted dwarf galaxy. However, its parent host remains uncertain. Here, we investigate a scenario in which Sequoia, Thamnos, and Gaia--Enceladus (GE) are debris from a single disrupted progenitor, the $ω\,$Dwarf, whose nucleus survives today as $ω\,$Cen. Using APOGEE and GALAH abundances together with Gaia astrometry, we reconstruct the chemical structure across this progenitor adopting orbital energy as a proxy for pre-merger radius. We find that the chemically evolved (younger Al-N-He-rich) population is strongly concentrated toward the inner regions, representing a population formed after/during the merger, while the primordial population represents a dwarf-galaxy-like population, supporting a common dwarf-galaxy origin for its components. The metallicity profile shows an inverted U-shaped gradient similar to those observed in present-day nucleated dwarf galaxies. At the same time, the inner regions ($ω\,$Cen+Thamnos) are more $α$-enhanced than the outskirts, pointing to shorter and more efficient star formation and indicating that the nucleus may have assembled through the merger of inspiraling globular clusters. Neutron-capture abundances reveal a Eu-rich, r-process-dominated outskirts and inner regions enhanced in [Ba/Eu] and [La/Eu], requiring delayed enrichment and more complex chemical evolution. Finally, our analysis shows that Sequoia and Thamnos naturally fit an outside-in stripping sequence around $ω\,$Cen, whereas the connection with GE remains unsure.

AI.tldr: Omega Centauri is proposed as the surviving nuclear star cluster of a disrupted dwarf galaxy progenitor called the ω Dwarf, with Sequoia and Thamnos debris representing outside-in stripping around ω Cen.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether Omega Centauri (ω Cen) is the surviving nuclear star cluster of a single disrupted dwarf galaxy progenitor, and to understand the connection between ω Cen and stellar debris streams (Sequoia, Thamnos, Gaia-Enceladus).
AI.method: Using APOGEE and GALAH chemical abundances combined with Gaia astrometry to reconstruct the chemical structure across the progenitor galaxy, adopting orbital energy as a proxy for pre-merger radius.
AI.result: The chemically evolved (Al-N-He-rich) population is concentrated in inner regions, representing post-merger formation, while the primordial population resembles a dwarf galaxy. The metallicity profile shows an inverted U-shaped gradient similar to nucleated dwarf galaxies. Inner regions (ω Cen+Thamnos) are more α-enhanced than outskirts, suggesting shorter, more efficient star formation. Neutron-capture abundances reveal r-process-dominated outskirts and inner regions enhanced in s-process elements, requiring delayed enrichment.
AI.conclusion: Sequoia and Thamnos fit an outside-in stripping sequence around ω Cen, supporting ω Cen as the nuclear star cluster of the ω Dwarf progenitor, though the connection with Gaia-Enceladus remains uncertain.

title: Deviations from the radial acceleration relation in the central galaxies of clusters, subclusters, and groups
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: Most galaxies closely follow the radial acceleration relation (RAR), which tightly links the observed accelerations to those predicted by Newtonian gravity from visible baryonic matter. Galaxy clusters, however, deviate from this relation. Several explanations have been proposed. Some of them predict that even some galaxies in clusters should deviate, but this hypothesis remains largely untested. We test it here by analyzing acceleration profiles for 17 early-type galaxies, derived from Jeans modeling of their globular cluster systems in our older work. Our sample spans central galaxies in clusters and groups, non-central galaxies, isolated ones, and-uniquelly for this paper-centrals in galactic subclusters, which are smaller clusters being accreted by larger ones. We compare these profiles to the standard RAR for non-cluster galaxies and its counterpart for clusters. We find that isolated and non-central galaxies adhere to the standard RAR. In contrast, central galaxies of clusters, subclusters, and groups exhibit enhanced accelerations in most cases, tracing instead the cluster acceleration behavior either partly or fully. The radius at which divergence from the standard RAR begins tends to decrease with increasing group mass. These findings imply that if cluster fields depart from the standard RAR due to undetected material, it must be dynamically cold and collisionless, such as non-baryonic cold dark matter, but also compact clouds of cold gas.

AI.tldr: Central galaxies in clusters, subclusters, and groups deviate from the standard radial acceleration relation (RAR) by showing enhanced accelerations, while isolated and non-central galaxies follow the standard RAR.
AI.motivation: To test whether galaxies in clusters deviate from the radial acceleration relation (RAR), as predicted by some explanations for why galaxy clusters themselves deviate from the RAR.
AI.method: Analyzed acceleration profiles for 17 early-type galaxies derived from Jeans modeling of their globular cluster systems. Sample included central galaxies in clusters/groups, non-central galaxies, isolated galaxies, and central galaxies in galactic subclusters. Compared these profiles to both the standard RAR for non-cluster galaxies and the cluster RAR.
AI.result: Isolated and non-central galaxies adhere to the standard RAR. Central galaxies of clusters, subclusters, and groups exhibit enhanced accelerations, tracing cluster acceleration behavior. The radius at which divergence from standard RAR begins decreases with increasing group mass.
AI.conclusion: If cluster fields depart from standard RAR due to undetected material, it must be dynamically cold and collisionless, such as non-baryonic cold dark matter or compact clouds of cold gas.

title: Direct Evidence for Stellar Initial Mass Function Variation in the Milky Way
summary: Because direct measurements require resolved stellar populations including low-mass stars, determining the stellar initial mass function (IMF) has been a historically difficult problem even within our own Galaxy and impossible everywhere else. As a result, even though it is predicted that the IMF should vary depending upon the properties of each individual star-forming molecular cloud, it is standard to assume a Universal IMF. Using recent observations from {\em Gaia}, it is now possible to test for IMF variation using resolved stellar populations in open clusters and a parameterization that separates properties of the IMF from subsequent dynamical evolution. Here, we show that the IMF is not Universal but instead varies across individual Galactic stellar populations, reflecting evolution in the average conditions of molecular clouds over cosmic time. This evolution is consistent with the predictions of a simple astrophysical model in which the IMF is environmentally-dependent and the Milky Way reflects typical galactic behavior in recent cosmic history. Thus, observational evidence now agrees with long-standing theoretical and numerical predictions.

AI.tldr: The IMF varies across Galactic stellar populations and evolves with cosmic time, contradicting the Universal IMF assumption and supporting environmentally-dependent IMF models.
AI.motivation: Historically, determining the IMF has been difficult due to the need for resolved stellar populations including low-mass stars. While theoretical predictions suggest IMF variation based on molecular cloud properties, the field has conventionally assumed a Universal IMF due to measurement limitations.
AI.method: Used recent Gaia observations of resolved stellar populations in open clusters with a parameterization that separates IMF properties from subsequent dynamical evolution. Applied a simple astrophysical model to test for IMF variation.
AI.result: The IMF is not Universal but varies across individual Galactic stellar populations. This variation reflects evolution in average molecular cloud conditions over cosmic time, consistent with environmentally-dependent IMF predictions.
AI.conclusion: Observational evidence now confirms long-standing theoretical predictions that the IMF varies with environment and evolves over cosmic time, with the Milky Way exhibiting typical galactic behavior in recent cosmic history.

title: Magnetic flux systems involved in the May 2024 solar energetic events from AR 13664 inferred through coronal dimmings
summary: Coronal dimmings are transient depletions of coronal plasma observed in extreme ultraviolet and soft X-rays, interpreted as low-corona signatures of coronal mass ejections (CMEs). Their evolution is closely linked to CME dynamics, flare reconnection, and large-scale coronal magnetic restructuring. In May 2024, active region (AR) 13664 produced 66 > M-class flares and multiple fast CMEs that caused the strongest geomagnetic storm since 2003. We analysed 16 on-disc dimmings observed in SDO/AIA 211Ådata between May 1-14, 2024. We extracted dimmings using logarithmic base-ratio thresholding, and derived magnetic properties from SDO/HMI line-of-sight magnetograms. We identified flare ribbons in AIA 1600Ådata via adaptive thresholding, and computed reconnection fluxes from radial magnetic field maps. We examined the magnetic flux systems involved in the eruptions using PFSS and NLFF extrapolations. We found correlations between dimming and flare ribbon properties stronger than previously reported. The dimming morphology evolved systematically, with southward expansion before May 9 and northward afterward, coinciding with a shift in flare ribbon locations between two mayor east-west polarity inversion lines (PILs). These changes imply the presence of two distinct magnetic domains. The PFSS extrapolations showed that southward (northward) dimmings are mainly strapping flux dimmings with magnetic field lines vaulting above the southern (northern) PIL. The final extent of the dimmings was then given by the exterior flux involved in the eruption via stretching and reconnection. One event revealed an extended quiet-Sun dimming likely triggered by EUV wave-induced field opening.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 16 coronal dimmings from AR 13664 in May 2024 reveals strong correlations between dimming and flare ribbon properties, systematic morphological evolution linked to magnetic domain shifts, and evidence of distinct strapping flux systems and quiet-Sun dimming triggered by EUV waves.
AI.motivation: To investigate the relationship between coronal dimmings, CME dynamics, flare reconnection, and magnetic restructuring during the intense solar activity from AR 13664 in May 2024, which produced numerous flares and CMEs causing the strongest geomagnetic storm since 2003.
AI.method: Analyzed 16 on-disc dimmings observed in SDO/AIA 211Å data from May 1-14, 2024 using logarithmic base-ratio thresholding for dimming extraction. Derived magnetic properties from SDO/HMI line-of-sight magnetograms. Identified flare ribbons in AIA 1600Å data via adaptive thresholding and computed reconnection fluxes from radial magnetic field maps. Examined magnetic flux systems using PFSS and NLFF extrapolations.
AI.result: Found stronger correlations between dimming and flare ribbon properties than previously reported. Dimming morphology evolved systematically: southward expansion before May 9 and northward afterward, coinciding with flare ribbon location shifts between two major east-west polarity inversion lines. PFSS extrapolations showed southward dimmings correspond to strapping flux vaulting above the southern PIL, while northward dimmings correspond to strapping flux above the northern PIL. Final dimming extent determined by exterior flux involved in eruption via stretching and reconnection. One event revealed extended quiet-Sun dimming likely triggered by EUV wave-induced field opening.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that coronal dimmings from AR 13664 reveal two distinct magnetic domains with systematic morphological evolution linked to magnetic restructuring. Dimming properties correlate strongly with flare ribbon characteristics, and dimming extent is determined by exterior flux involvement in eruptions. The findings provide insights into magnetic flux systems involved in CME eruptions and their relationship to flare reconnection processes.

title: The impact of disc disruption on Milky Way satellite counts
comment: 12 pages, 7 figures. To be submitted to MNRAS
summary: Estimates for the total number of Milky Way (MW) satellites are often generated from a combination of the observed number of satellites in surveys, adjustments for the completeness of those surveys, and theoretical expectations from halo assembly modelling. One of the features of this modelling is disruption by the MW stellar disc. We examine the effect of degrees of disc disruption on inferred satellite counts, by means of an N-body simulation of a MW-mass halo plus a toy model for this disruption. We use a fictional all-sky survey to show that high resilience to disc disruption predicts small populations of satellites that are radially very concentrated around the central galaxy and are hosted by massive subhaloes, while low resilience predicts many more satellites with a less concentrated radial distribution and hosted within less massive subhaloes. We show that the most massive subhaloes are particularly susceptible to disruption due to their radial orbits, and in their putative absence galaxy formation must occur in lower mass haloes that have a shallower radial number density profile. We then demonstrate this phenomenon for a combination of the Pan-STARRS and DES surveys. It is therefore necessary to account for uncertainty in the disc disruption radius when making predictions for MW satellite distributions.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how varying degrees of Milky Way disc disruption affect inferred satellite galaxy counts, showing that disruption models significantly impact predicted satellite populations and radial distributions.
AI.motivation: To address uncertainty in Milky Way satellite population estimates caused by varying assumptions about stellar disc disruption effects, which are crucial for accurate satellite count predictions.
AI.method: Uses N-body simulations of a MW-mass halo plus a toy model for disc disruption, combined with fictional all-sky surveys and analysis of Pan-STARRS and DES survey data.
AI.result: High disc disruption resilience predicts small, radially concentrated satellite populations in massive subhaloes, while low resilience predicts many more satellites with shallower radial distributions in less massive subhaloes. Most massive subhaloes are particularly susceptible to disruption due to radial orbits.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty in disc disruption radius must be accounted for when making predictions for Milky Way satellite distributions, as disruption models significantly affect inferred satellite populations and their properties.

title: Thermally inflated accretors in post-mass transfer binaries: Abell 35 and its class revisited
comment: 20 pages (including appendix), 2 tables, 16 figures. Submitted to PASP. Comments are welcome! All codes and MESA inlists can be found in https://github.com/Soumin1908/InflatedStars_A35_MesaModels.git
summary: A small but growing class of binaries containing hot ($T_{\rm eff}\sim10^5\rm~K$) white dwarfs (WDs) and rapidly rotating, apparently subgiant companions -- including the prototype, Abell 35 -- show companions that are too large and luminous to be ordinary main-sequence stars yet too numerous to be explained as finely tuned near-twin binaries. We argue that these stars are instead main-sequence accretors temporarily inflated out of thermal equilibrium by recent mass transfer. For the subgiant of Abell 35, a new Gaia DR3 astrometric orbit ($P_{\rm orb} = 790$ d) combined with updated photometric and spectroscopic constraints yield $T_{\rm eff} \approx 4900~\rm K$, $R \approx 3~R_{\odot}$, near-solar metallicity, and rapid rotation aligned with the orbit ($v_{\rm rot} \approx 195~\rm km~s^{-1}$), indicating substantial recent accretion and spin-up. Dynamical mass limits disfavor a coeval twin-binary origin, supporting the inflated-accretor interpretation. We test this scenario using self-consistent MESA binary evolution calculations with a new accretion prescription in which accreted material retains a fraction of its infall energy. The accretor expands to giant-like radii when $\dot{M}$ is high yet remains within its Roche lobe, allowing stable mass transfer even for mass ratios traditionally considered unstable. After mass transfer ceases, the star contracts on Myr timescales through a bloated, rapidly rotating phase whose temperatures, radii, and spins match those observed in Abell 35-type systems. This framework explains the population without fine tuning and unifies Abell 35-type binaries with post-AGB binaries, blue lurkers, and wide WD$+$main-sequence systems as successive stages of the same post-mass-transfer evolutionary pathway.

AI.tldr: Hot white dwarf binaries with inflated, rapidly rotating companions are explained as main-sequence accretors temporarily bloated by recent mass transfer, not as finely-tuned twin binaries.
AI.motivation: To explain a puzzling class of binaries containing hot white dwarfs with companions that appear too large and luminous for main-sequence stars yet too numerous to be finely-tuned near-twin binaries, particularly the prototype Abell 35 system.
AI.method: Combined Gaia DR3 astrometric orbit data with photometric and spectroscopic constraints for Abell 35, then tested the inflated-accretor scenario using MESA binary evolution calculations with a new accretion prescription where accreted material retains a fraction of its infall energy.
AI.result: Abell 35's companion has T_eff ≈ 4900 K, R ≈ 3 R⊙, near-solar metallicity, rapid rotation (v_rot ≈ 195 km/s) aligned with orbit, indicating recent accretion and spin-up. MESA simulations show accretors expand to giant-like radii during high mass transfer, then contract on Myr timescales through bloated, rapidly rotating phases matching observed systems.
AI.conclusion: The inflated-accretor framework explains Abell 35-type binaries without fine tuning and unifies them with post-AGB binaries, blue lurkers, and wide WD+main-sequence systems as successive stages of the same post-mass-transfer evolutionary pathway.

title: II- A hydrodynamical CLONE of the Virgo cluster to confront observed and synthetic galaxy population twins in a dense environment
comment: Submitted since August 2025, 13 pages+appendixes, 8 figures in the core text + 5 figures in the appendixes
summary: Galaxy clusters offer powerful laboratories for studying galaxy evolution in dense environments. In this context, the CLONE, Constrained LOcal and Nesting Environment, project provides a zoom-in hydrodynamical simulation of the Virgo cluster, including AGN and supernovae feedback, with a resolution down to 350 pc, designed to mirror Virgo's observed properties. Previous work showed that this replica and Virgo share the same history, mass and luminosity distributions including the central M87. This study examines several observational relations extending to lower stellar masses than previous synthetic-population studies: star formation density, (specific) star formation rate, metallicity and quenched fraction of galaxies as a function of stellar mass and cluster-centric distance. The aim is to assess how simulated and observed trends compare. Despite slightly low metallicity and high, but then enough, quenched fraction, simulated galaxies reproduce key observational trends even without averaging or accounting for observational uncertainties, aside from the consideration of projection effects: At fixed stellar mass, cluster galaxies form fewer stars than field counterparts. Most galaxies are quenched but for those of intermediate mass or isolated. Low-mass galaxies are highly quenched implying a sharp metallicity drop, and low metallicity does not imply youth. Quenching occurs earlier for the most massive and the smallest galaxies than for those of intermediate mass at least until they enter the cluster. Quenched galaxies have undergone dark matter stripping. Gas depletion drives quenching, especially in low-mass galaxies and the farther from the cluster center they are. Overall, the synthetic population reproduces jointly multiple observational trends, making it a valuable tool to probe processes from jellyfish galaxies to cluster-core gas dynamics. [Shorten]

AI.tldr: The CLONE project's Virgo cluster simulation reproduces key observational trends in galaxy properties including star formation, metallicity, and quenching across stellar mass ranges, validating it as a tool for studying environmental effects.
AI.motivation: To assess how well hydrodynamical simulations with AGN and supernova feedback can reproduce observed galaxy properties in cluster environments, particularly extending analysis to lower stellar masses than previous synthetic-population studies.
AI.method: Uses the CLONE project's zoom-in hydrodynamical simulation of the Virgo cluster with 350 pc resolution, including AGN and supernova feedback, designed to match Virgo's observed properties. Analyzes star formation density, specific star formation rate, metallicity, and quenched fraction as functions of stellar mass and cluster-centric distance.
AI.result: Simulated galaxies reproduce key observational trends: cluster galaxies form fewer stars than field counterparts at fixed mass; most galaxies are quenched except intermediate-mass or isolated ones; low-mass galaxies show high quenching with sharp metallicity drops; quenching occurs earlier for most massive and smallest galaxies; quenched galaxies undergo dark matter stripping; gas depletion drives quenching, especially in low-mass galaxies farther from cluster center.
AI.conclusion: The synthetic population successfully reproduces multiple observational trends simultaneously, making the CLONE simulation a valuable tool for probing environmental processes from jellyfish galaxies to cluster-core gas dynamics, despite minor discrepancies in metallicity and quenched fraction.

title: The Role of Reconnection at Magnetic Separators in Complex Solar Flare Ribbons
comment: 15 pages, 9 figures, Astrophysical Journal in press
summary: Solar flare ribbons, manifesting as transient brightenings in the chromosphere, are believed to trace out the footpoints of magnetic field lines that are reconnecting higher in the solar atmosphere. These field lines lie in a separatrix or quasi-separatrix layer that separates domains of different magnetic connectivity and hence forms a natural location for reconnection. Solar flares are typically characterized as being circular ribbon flares, two-ribbon flares, or complex ribbon flares based on the number and shape of the ribbons. There are relatively well-developed models to explain the first two types of flares based on the location of the reconnection powering the flare. The case of complex ribbons is less well understood, but is often posited to be a result of reconnection at multiple locations. We demonstrate here that reconnection at a magnetic separator connecting two coronal null points can naturally explain the complex ribbons observed for two events, an M2.9 class flare from NOAA AR 11112 (SOL2010-10-16T19:12), and an X2.2 class flare from NOAA AR 11158 (SOL2011-02-15T01:56).

AI.tldr: Complex solar flare ribbons can be explained by reconnection at magnetic separators connecting two coronal null points, as demonstrated for two specific flare events.
AI.motivation: While circular and two-ribbon flares have well-developed models based on reconnection location, complex ribbon flares are less understood and often attributed to multiple reconnection sites. This paper aims to provide a specific mechanism for complex ribbon formation.
AI.method: The authors propose that reconnection at a magnetic separator connecting two coronal null points can naturally explain complex ribbons. They demonstrate this mechanism using two specific flare events: an M2.9 class flare from NOAA AR 11112 (SOL2010-10-16T19:12) and an X2.2 class flare from NOAA AR 11158 (SOL2011-02-15T01:56).
AI.result: The study demonstrates that the complex ribbon patterns observed in both flare events can be explained by reconnection occurring at magnetic separators connecting two coronal null points, providing a unified mechanism for complex ribbon formation.
AI.conclusion: Complex solar flare ribbons, previously poorly understood, can be explained by reconnection at magnetic separators connecting coronal null points, offering a specific physical mechanism for this type of flare morphology.

title: Revealing the nature of the starburst galaxies in the $z=2.4$ overdensity HATLAS J0849
comment: 25 pages. 12 figures. Preprint. Submitted to MNRAS on March 18; under peer review. Constructive comments welcome
summary: Today's most massive ellipticals are proposed to originate from starbursting galaxies in $z\gtrsim2$ overdensities. To discern what triggers these starbursts, and their $z=0$ descendants, we performed a detailed case study of five gas-rich galaxies in the $z=2.41$ overdensity, HATLAS J084933.4+021443. Using 0.15" resolution CO(4-3), [C I] 1-0, and dust-continuum observations, we characterised their cold gas morphology and kinematics. We find two rotating discs, W and C, both exhibiting non-axisymmetric radial gas motions (consistent with bars). Of the two extreme starbursts, W is a lopsided, rotation-dominated disc with a rotation velocity of $\sim520$ km s$^{-1}$, whereas T is most likely a late-stage merger. Combined with recent studies, we find that $\gtrsim42\%$ of gas-rich, massive starbursts in overdensities are rotation-dominated discs, a fraction not yet systematically reproduced by galaxy evolution models. Beyond $z=1$, disc galaxies with rotation velocities of $>400$ km s$^{-1}$ reside almost exclusively in overdensities, consistent with early mass assembly in dense environments. By comparing to local early-type galaxies with cold gas discs, we confirm that these systems already reside in halos comparable to the most massive $z\sim0$ ellipticals at the centres of groups and clusters. Despite their extreme star-formation rates, these discs lie on the same $σ-$SFR locus as lower-SFR field galaxies, implying that stellar feedback remains the dominant turbulence driver. We postulate that this is because inflowing gas is effectively transported through ordered streaming, such that only a small fraction of kinetic energy feeds disc-wide turbulence.

AI.tldr: High-resolution observations of gas-rich galaxies in a z=2.41 overdensity reveal rotating discs with extreme kinematics, suggesting early mass assembly in dense environments and challenging current galaxy evolution models.
AI.motivation: To understand what triggers extreme starbursts in high-redshift overdensities and identify their z=0 descendants, particularly focusing on the formation mechanisms of today's most massive elliptical galaxies.
AI.method: Detailed case study of five gas-rich galaxies in the z=2.41 overdensity HATLAS J084933.4+021443 using 0.15" resolution CO(4-3), [C I] 1-0, and dust-continuum observations to characterize cold gas morphology and kinematics.
AI.result: Found two rotating discs (W and C) with non-axisymmetric radial gas motions consistent with bars. W is a lopsided, rotation-dominated disc with v_rot~520 km/s, while T is likely a late-stage merger. ≥42% of gas-rich massive starbursts in overdensities are rotation-dominated discs. Discs with v_rot>400 km/s beyond z=1 reside almost exclusively in overdensities. These systems already reside in halos comparable to the most massive z~0 ellipticals at group/cluster centers.
AI.conclusion: Extreme starburst discs in overdensities represent early mass assembly in dense environments, challenging galaxy evolution models. Despite high SFRs, they follow the same σ-SFR relation as lower-SFR field galaxies, indicating stellar feedback remains the dominant turbulence driver, with inflowing gas efficiently transported via ordered streaming motions.

title: A generalized method for estimating solar wind speeds and densities using spectral broadening for a Kolmogorov turbulence spectrum
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, Accepted for publication in Advances in Space Research
summary: We present a unified method to derive both solar wind velocities and coronal electron densities in the near-Sun corona using Doppler spectral broadening of spacecraft radio signals. The method is generalized to be frequency independent under the assumption that electron density fluctuations follow a Kolmogorov spectrum. We validate the approach using S-band data from India's Mars Orbiter Mission during the October 2021 superior conjunction at 5-8 R$_\odot$, and X-band data from Japan's Akatsuki during June 2016 and October 2022 conjunctions spanning 1.4-10 R$_\odot$. From S-band we obtained wind speeds of 100-150 km s$^{-1}$ and electron densities of order $10^{10}$ m$^{-3}$. X-band results show speeds ranging from $\sim$150 km s$^{-1}$ near the equator to $\sim$400 km s$^{-1}$ in coronal-hole regions, with consistent radial trends in density. We provide a compact, frequency-scaled relation that maps Doppler spectral width to both $v$ and $N_e$. The formulation enables consistent application across telecommunication bands and complements in-situ probes for coronal plasma studies.

AI.tldr: A unified method for deriving solar wind velocities and coronal electron densities from Doppler spectral broadening of spacecraft radio signals, validated with S-band and X-band data from Mars Orbiter Mission and Akatsuki missions.
AI.motivation: To develop a consistent, frequency-independent method for extracting both solar wind speeds and electron densities from radio signal Doppler broadening during spacecraft superior conjunctions, complementing in-situ measurements for coronal plasma studies.
AI.method: Generalized frequency-independent approach assuming Kolmogorov spectrum for electron density fluctuations, applied to S-band data from India's Mars Orbiter Mission (October 2021, 5-8 R⊙) and X-band data from Japan's Akatsuki (June 2016, October 2022, 1.4-10 R⊙).
AI.result: S-band: wind speeds 100-150 km/s, electron densities ~10¹⁰ m⁻³. X-band: speeds ~150 km/s near equator to ~400 km/s in coronal-hole regions, with consistent radial density trends. Developed compact frequency-scaled relation mapping Doppler spectral width to both velocity and density.
AI.conclusion: The unified formulation enables consistent application across telecommunication bands and provides complementary remote sensing capability to in-situ probes for comprehensive coronal plasma characterization.

title: SPHEREx mapping of diffuse PAH and H II emission in the Galactic plane
comment: 15 pages, 9 figures
summary: We present preliminary SPHEREx maps of diffuse Galactic emission tracing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and ionized hydrogen gas, and we study their relationship across the Galactic plane. Since its launch in early 2025, the SPHEREx space telescope has been conducting an all-sky near-infrared spectral survey from 0.75 to 5.0 microns. We produce a large-scale map of the 3.3-micron PAH emission feature, which is bright and detectable throughout the Galactic plane, and find a strong correlation with the thermal dust radiance measured by Planck. We also trace ionized hydrogen gas by producing a map of Brackett-alpha emission at 4.05 microns. By combining the two maps, we identify extended shells of PAH emission associated with photodissociation regions surrounding ionized gas. We construct a PAH abundance map and find a significant anticorrelation between PAH abundance and ionized hydrogen, indicating systematic PAH depletion within ionized gas regions across the Galactic plane and demonstrating that ionizing radiation is a dominant driver of PAH abundance variations. These early SPHEREx results provide a large-scale view of PAHs and ionized hydrogen and preview the capability of the mission to map diffuse emission in the interstellar medium.

AI.tldr: SPHEREx maps reveal strong correlation between 3.3μm PAH emission and thermal dust, and anticorrelation between PAH abundance and ionized hydrogen gas across the Galactic plane, showing ionizing radiation drives PAH depletion.
AI.motivation: To study the relationship between polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and ionized hydrogen gas across the Galactic plane using early SPHEREx observations, understanding how ionizing radiation affects PAH abundance in the interstellar medium.
AI.method: Used SPHEREx all-sky near-infrared spectral survey data (0.75-5.0μm) to create large-scale maps of 3.3μm PAH emission and 4.05μm Brackett-alpha emission tracing ionized hydrogen gas, then combined these maps to analyze correlations and construct PAH abundance maps.
AI.result: Found strong correlation between 3.3μm PAH emission and Planck thermal dust radiance; identified extended PAH shells in photodissociation regions; discovered significant anticorrelation between PAH abundance and ionized hydrogen, indicating systematic PAH depletion within ionized gas regions.
AI.conclusion: Ionizing radiation is a dominant driver of PAH abundance variations across the Galactic plane, with PAHs being systematically depleted in ionized gas regions; early SPHEREx results demonstrate the mission's capability to map diffuse interstellar medium emission.

title: A Catalog of 1,408 Carbon-Enhanced Metal-Poor Stars from LAMOST DR11
summary: Metal-poor (MP) stars are important targets for investigating the chemical evolution of the early universe. Among them, Carbon-Enhanced Metal-Poor (CEMP) stars have attracted extensive attention due to their rarity and astrophysical significance. Owing to their low occurrence rate, the identification of MP stars and CEMP stars remains a task of considerable scientific value. In this study, we investigate the search for CEMP stars based on the low-resolution stellar spectra from LAMOST (Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope) DR11 and propose a deep-learning-based approach for this purpose. By analyzing the LAMOST DR11 spectral library, we identify 1,408 CEMP star candidates. For ease of reference and further use, we provide the estimated stellar parameters for these objects, including $T_\texttt{eff}$, $\log~g$, [Fe/H], and [C/H].

AI.tldr: A deep learning approach identifies 1,408 Carbon-Enhanced Metal-Poor star candidates from LAMOST DR11 low-resolution spectra, providing their stellar parameters.
AI.motivation: Metal-poor stars, especially Carbon-Enhanced Metal-Poor stars, are rare but crucial for studying early universe chemical evolution. Their low occurrence rate makes identification challenging but scientifically valuable.
AI.method: Developed a deep-learning-based approach to search for CEMP stars using low-resolution stellar spectra from LAMOST DR11. Analyzed the spectral library to identify candidates.
AI.result: Identified 1,408 CEMP star candidates from LAMOST DR11. Provided estimated stellar parameters including effective temperature (Teff), surface gravity (log g), metallicity ([Fe/H]), and carbon abundance ([C/H]) for these objects.
AI.conclusion: The deep learning approach successfully identifies CEMP star candidates from large spectroscopic surveys, facilitating further study of these rare objects important for understanding early galactic chemical evolution.

title: Large Variations Seen in First Ultraviolet Spectroscopic M33 Dust Extinction Curves
comment: ApJ, in press; 12 pages, 7 figures
summary: Dust extinction curves provide one of the main avenues to understanding the detailed nature of dust grains and accounting for the effects of dust on observations of many astrophysical objects. For the first time, spectroscopic ultraviolet (UV) extinction curves are measured in M33 expanding the sample of Local Group galaxies with such measurements to five. These curves are based on Hubble Space Telescope/Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph spectra and literature photometry from the UV to the near-infrared. The four measured curves show large variations in their UV shapes including their 2175 A bump and UV slope strengths. The average extinction of these four sightlines is lower than the averages for other Local Group Galaxies and does not follow the Milky Way R(V) dependent relationship. The variations between UV extinction shape parameters and gas-to-dust ratios for the M33 sightlines fall within the variations seen in the combined sample of UV extinction curves in the Milky Way, Large and Small Magellanic Clouds, and M31. The correlation with gas-to-dust ratio is much stronger than the correlation with global metallicity. This strengthens the picture that local conditions like radiation field density and shocks dominate over global galaxy properties like metallicity in determining the wavelength dependence of dust extinction.

AI.tldr: First UV extinction curves in M33 show large variations in UV shape parameters, with average extinction lower than other Local Group galaxies and not following Milky Way R(V) relationships.
AI.motivation: To expand understanding of dust extinction properties beyond the Milky Way and Magellanic Clouds by measuring UV extinction curves in M33, increasing the sample of Local Group galaxies with such measurements to five.
AI.method: Used Hubble Space Telescope/Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph spectra combined with literature photometry from UV to near-infrared to measure spectroscopic UV extinction curves for four sightlines in M33.
AI.result: The four M33 extinction curves show large variations in UV shapes (2175 Å bump and UV slope strengths), with average extinction lower than other Local Group galaxies and not following Milky Way R(V) relationships. Variations correlate more strongly with gas-to-dust ratio than global metallicity.
AI.conclusion: Local conditions (radiation field density, shocks) dominate over global galaxy properties (metallicity) in determining dust extinction wavelength dependence, as evidenced by stronger correlation with gas-to-dust ratio than metallicity across Local Group galaxies.

title: ZIMPOL detects scattering polarization in He I D$_3$ during a solar flare
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary: Spectropolarimetric observations of solar flares in the He I D$_3$ line at 5876 Å are extremely rare, and their diagnostic potential remains largely unexplored. We report the first unambiguous detection of linear polarization in He I D$_3$ during a solar flare. Using the high-precision ZIMPOL polarimeter at the IRSOL observatory in Locarno (Switzerland), we tracked the temporal evolution of the He I D$_3$ Stokes profiles throughout the M7 GOES-class flare that occurred on 3 May 2023 at 10:45 UT. We analyzed the time evolution of the maximum in linear polarization and the absorption depth of the intensity profile. Both the fractional linear polarization, which peaks at $6\times10^{-4}$, and the absorption depth increase rapidly before gradually decaying, with their maxima occurring approximately 5 minutes after the peaks in GOES X-ray flux and SDO/AIA 304 Å emission. From the evolving He I D$_3$ core position, we also derived the temporal evolution of the plasma bulk velocities. The intensity profiles exhibiting strong absorption seems to originate from the flare ribbons. The time evolution of all Stokes parameters in the 3 May 2023 event was driven by changes in ortho-helium density prompted by the different phases of the flare. Our analysis suggests that the observed He I D$_3$ linear polarization is likely not dominated by the theorized impact polarization, as it exhibits neither spatial correspondence with electron precipitation sites nor temporal synchronization with the impulsive phase. Instead, the signals are consistent with scattering polarization produced by anisotropic radiation pumping. We conclude that scattering polarization signal on the order of $0.01\%$ can be produced in the He I D$_3$ line during solar flares. This can provide constraints for flare models.

AI.tldr: First detection of linear polarization in He I D₃ line during solar flare, showing scattering polarization rather than impact polarization, with 0.01% signal providing constraints for flare models.
AI.motivation: Spectropolarimetric observations of solar flares in He I D₃ line are extremely rare and their diagnostic potential remains largely unexplored, motivating this first unambiguous detection study.
AI.method: Used high-precision ZIMPOL polarimeter at IRSOL observatory to track temporal evolution of He I D₃ Stokes profiles during M7 GOES-class flare on 3 May 2023; analyzed linear polarization maximum, absorption depth, and derived plasma bulk velocities from line core positions.
AI.result: Detected fractional linear polarization peaking at 6×10⁻⁴ (0.06%); polarization and absorption depth maxima occurred ~5 minutes after GOES X-ray and AIA 304 Å peaks; signals consistent with scattering polarization from anisotropic radiation pumping rather than impact polarization.
AI.conclusion: Scattering polarization signals on order of 0.01% can be produced in He I D₃ line during solar flares, providing constraints for flare models; observed polarization not dominated by theorized impact polarization but by scattering from anisotropic radiation pumping.

title: SS Cygni: An analysis of quasi-periodic oscillations in the range of 0.25h to 8h
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures
summary: Presented here is an analysis of over 66,000 magnitude measurements, made by the author, of the cataclysmic variable SS Cygni in two photometric filters (Cousins R and Johnson V). This large number of measurements, along with their cadence, is sufficient to analyse the light curve for quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) ranging from a few minutes to a few hours with the most frequently found QPOs having periods of 30 minutes (48 cycles/day). These QPOs occur predominantly during quiescent phases rather than outbursts, representing a previously understudied aspect of SS Cygni's variability.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 66,000+ magnitude measurements of SS Cygni reveals quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) with 30-minute periods predominantly during quiescent phases, representing a previously understudied aspect of this cataclysmic variable's variability.
AI.motivation: To systematically analyze the light curve of SS Cygni for quasi-periodic oscillations across a wide range of timescales (minutes to hours) using an unprecedented dataset of over 66,000 magnitude measurements, particularly focusing on previously understudied variability aspects during quiescent phases.
AI.method: The author conducted extensive photometric observations of SS Cygni using two filters (Cousins R and Johnson V), collecting over 66,000 magnitude measurements with sufficient cadence to analyze quasi-periodic oscillations across timescales from minutes to hours, focusing on identifying periodic signals in the light curve.
AI.result: The analysis revealed quasi-periodic oscillations with the most frequently found period of 30 minutes (48 cycles/day), occurring predominantly during quiescent phases rather than outbursts, identifying a previously understudied aspect of SS Cygni's variability.
AI.conclusion: SS Cygni exhibits significant quasi-periodic oscillations with characteristic 30-minute periods that are preferentially associated with quiescent phases, providing new insights into the variability mechanisms of this cataclysmic binary system beyond the well-studied outburst behavior.

title: Stellar Variability and Distance Indicators in the Near-infrared in Nearby Galaxies. II. Pulsating Stars in the Carina Dwarf Spheroidal
comment: AJ accepted, 14 pages, 4 Tables and 10 Figures
summary: We present homogeneous, near-infrared ($JHK_s$ bands) time-series observations of the classical Carina dwarf Spheroidal (dSph) galaxy to determine accurate and precise distances using the pulsating stars as standard candles. These observations cover two Carina dSph fields ($\sim10.8'\times10.8'$) obtained with the FourStar infrared camera mounted on the 6.5-m Magellan Telescope. We collected precise photometric measurements of 43 RR Lyrae, 11 anomalous Cepheids (ACep), and 102 dwarf Cepheids (DCep) in Carina dSph. Using RR Lyrae, we obtained a distance modulus of $20.079\pm0.028\mathrm{(statistical)}\pm0.045\mathrm{(systematic)}$~mag, or a distance to Carina of $103.7\pm1.3\mathrm{(statistical)}\pm2.2\mathrm{(systematic)}$~kpc. The literature calibrations based on SX Phoenicis or delta-Scuti stars were used to anchor the $JHK_s$ period-luminosity relations for DCep. This resulted in a distance modulus that is in excellent agreement with RR Lyrae based determination. Finally, the distance moduli estimates using the ACep were found to be systematically smaller than the RR Lyrae-based distance modulus, suggesting a metallicity dependence on the ACep period-luminosity relation.

AI.tldr: Near-infrared time-series observations of Carina dwarf spheroidal galaxy used to determine precise distances via pulsating stars (RR Lyrae, anomalous Cepheids, dwarf Cepheids) as standard candles.
AI.motivation: To obtain accurate and precise distances to the Carina dwarf spheroidal galaxy using pulsating stars as standard candles, particularly focusing on improving distance measurements through homogeneous near-infrared observations.
AI.method: Used FourStar infrared camera on 6.5-m Magellan Telescope to collect JHKs-band time-series photometry of two Carina dSph fields. Analyzed 43 RR Lyrae, 11 anomalous Cepheids, and 102 dwarf Cepheids. Applied period-luminosity relations for distance determination, with literature calibrations for dwarf Cepheids.
AI.result: Obtained distance modulus of 20.079±0.028(stat)±0.045(sys) mag (103.7±1.3±2.2 kpc) from RR Lyrae. Dwarf Cepheid distances agreed excellently with RR Lyrae results. Anomalous Cepheid distances were systematically smaller, suggesting metallicity dependence in their period-luminosity relation.
AI.conclusion: Homogeneous near-infrared observations provide precise distance measurements to Carina dSph. RR Lyrae and dwarf Cepheids give consistent distances, while anomalous Cepheid results indicate metallicity effects on their period-luminosity calibration, highlighting the importance of metallicity considerations for certain standard candles.

title: Estimation of the magnetic field strength from ALMA dust polarization in the protocluster G327.29
comment: Submitted to A&A journal
summary: Magnetic fields and turbulence may play a crucial role in the evolution of molecular clouds and ultimately in the formation of dense cores and stars. Despite being studied in many molecular clouds, the exact role of magnetic fields and turbulence in star formation is still poorly understood. Here, we report the high resolution plane of sky magnetic field (B_pos) morphology toward the high mass star forming region G327.29, obtained with the 12-meter of the Atacama Large Millimeter/sub-millimeter Array (ALMA) telescope. From our analysis, we obtain a complex B_pos morphology where the magnetic field orientation is uniformly distributed across the entire range from -90 to +90 deg. The observed area is composed of one filament and one dense central clump, which harbor multiple dense cores. The total magnetic field strengths (B_tot) in these regions are 1.4 \pm 0.7 mG and 2.0 \pm 0.8 mG at a number density (n) of 6.8 \pm 1.5 x 10^5 and 1.1 \pm 0.3 x 10^6 cm^-3 , derived from the angular dispersion function (ADF) method. The virial parameters (α vir )in these regions are 7.7 \pm 7.1 and 0.7 \pm 0.6, suggesting that the regions may be gravitationally bound or unbound after accounting for the errors. Moreover, the ratio of turbulent to magnetic energy (~ 0.25) indicates that the magnetic field is dynamically more important than turbulence. The relative influence of turbulence and magnetic fields on core dynamics appears to depend on how the B_tot scales with gas density (\r{ho}) in the densest regions. In summary, this work presents a comprehensive analysis of the relative roles of magnetic fields, turbulence, and gravity in regulating high-mass star formation in G327.29, enabled by high-resolution ALMA observations.

AI.tldr: High-resolution ALMA observations of high-mass star-forming region G327.29 reveal complex magnetic field morphology with uniform orientation distribution, magnetic field strengths of 1.4-2.0 mG at high densities, and magnetic energy dominating over turbulence.
AI.motivation: Despite extensive study, the exact roles of magnetic fields and turbulence in star formation remain poorly understood, particularly in high-mass star-forming regions. This study aims to characterize these forces in G327.29 using high-resolution observations.
AI.method: Used ALMA 12-meter telescope for high-resolution plane-of-sky magnetic field morphology observations. Applied angular dispersion function (ADF) method to derive total magnetic field strengths. Analyzed virial parameters and energy ratios to assess gravitational binding and relative importance of magnetic fields vs. turbulence.
AI.result: Complex B_pos morphology with uniform orientation distribution (-90 to +90°). Magnetic field strengths: 1.4±0.7 mG at n=6.8±1.5×10⁵ cm⁻³ and 2.0±0.8 mG at n=1.1±0.3×10⁶ cm⁻³. Virial parameters: 7.7±7.1 and 0.7±0.6. Turbulent-to-magnetic energy ratio ~0.25, indicating magnetic fields dominate over turbulence.
AI.conclusion: Magnetic fields are dynamically more important than turbulence in regulating high-mass star formation in G327.29. The relative influence of turbulence and magnetic fields on core dynamics depends on how magnetic field strength scales with gas density in dense regions.

title: Unlocking HST's Stellar Treasure Trove: Stellar Activity Minima for HAT-P-11 Offer Prime Windows for Transmission Spectroscopy
comment: 19 Pages, 11 Figures. Comments welcome
summary: HAT-P-11 is a well-studied, active K dwarf hosting an eccentric, misaligned transiting sub-Neptune. As part of the HST Stellar Treasure Trove program (HST-AR-17551), we analyze absolutely calibrated out-of-transit \HST{} spectra from \texttt{STIS} and \texttt{WFC3} across the \textsc{G430L}, \textsc{G750L}, \textsc{G102}, and \textsc{G141} bandpasses to constrain the surface heterogeneity of HAT-P-11 and its impact on transmission spectroscopy. Grid-based spectral retrievals using NewEra \texttt{PHOENIX} models robustly favor two-component photospheres in the \texttt{WFC3} G102 and G141 data, with a ${\sim}4950$\,K photospheric component and a cooler ($\sim$3400\,K) component covering 26{--}33\% of the stellar disk. By contrast, retrievals on the \texttt{STIS} optical spectra do not yield a satisfactory fit, reflecting current limitations of stellar atmosphere models in the optical regime compared to the \HST{} observational precision. We contextualize these results using long-term photometric monitoring and chromospheric activity indices. The inferred high spot covering fractions are broadly consistent with the elevated photometric variability observed during the \textit{Kepler} era ($f_{\rm spot}$$\sim$10--20\%) but are in tension with the much lower rotational amplitudes observed from TESS in the mid 2020s ($f_{\rm spot}$$\sim$1--10\%). This secular decline in variability is mirrored by a $\sim$20\% decrease in the Ca\,\textsc{ii} H\&K index. These results imply that HAT-P-11 undergoes comparatively quiescent phases that offer more favorable windows for atmospheric characterization, which serendipitously coincided with some of the recent JWST observations. More generally, our study demonstrates that multi-epoch, space-based stellar spectra provides a physically grounded pathway for mitigating stellar contamination in high-precision transmission spectra in the JWST era.

AI.tldr: HST spectra reveal HAT-P-11 has significant surface heterogeneity with a two-component photosphere (4950K and 3400K components), but stellar models struggle to fit optical data. The star shows declining activity from Kepler to TESS eras, suggesting quiescent phases that benefit exoplanet atmospheric studies.
AI.motivation: To characterize stellar surface heterogeneity of active K dwarf HAT-P-11 and understand its impact on transmission spectroscopy, particularly for the eccentric, misaligned transiting sub-Neptune it hosts.
AI.method: Analyzed absolutely calibrated out-of-transit HST spectra from STIS and WFC3 across multiple bandpasses (G430L, G750L, G102, G141). Used grid-based spectral retrievals with NewEra PHOENIX models to constrain photospheric components. Contextualized results with long-term photometric monitoring and chromospheric activity indices.
AI.result: WFC3 data robustly favor two-component photospheres: ~4950K photospheric component and cooler ~3400K component covering 26-33% of stellar disk. STIS optical spectra retrievals unsatisfactory, revealing limitations of stellar atmosphere models in optical regime. Spot covering fractions consistent with Kepler-era photometric variability but in tension with lower TESS rotational amplitudes, indicating secular decline in activity mirrored by ~20% decrease in Ca II H&K index.
AI.conclusion: HAT-P-11 undergoes quiescent phases that offer more favorable windows for atmospheric characterization, coinciding with recent JWST observations. Multi-epoch, space-based stellar spectra provide a physically grounded pathway for mitigating stellar contamination in high-precision transmission spectroscopy in the JWST era.

title: The shortest detected intra-day variability of active galactic nuclei in TESS survey
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary: AGNs are known to be variable in almost all wavelengths and timescales. The shortest variability timescale of AGNs can be used to probe the smallest scale structures within AGNs. We aim to measure the shortest detected variability timescale, $t_{min,ul}$, of type 1 radio-quiet Seyfert galaxies and analyse their characteristics. We extracted TESS light curves of 47 Seyfert 1 galaxies. We measured the PSDs of the sample, modelled by a power law model plus a constant noise, and constrained the shortest detected AGN variability timescale as the power law component exceeds the constant noise and systematic uncertainties indicated by the upper limits of non-variable quiescent galaxies' PSDs. We measured the upper limits of the shortest variability timescale to be $\log(t_{min,ul}/hrs)=0.85\pm0.55$. We compared these upper limits to a range of theoretical AGN variability timescales, and the natural interpretation of our measured $t_{min,ul}$ is the light crossing scale from a coherently varying region, where the measured $t_{min,ul}$ corresponds to the range from a few to thousands of gravitational radii. A significant fraction of these light crossing scales is smaller than the accretion disk emission sizes measured by quasar microlensing, reverberation mapping, or theoretical accretion disk models. Since we only measure the upper limits, the true physical shortest variability timescales are even shorter. We measure the power law index to be $2.0\pm0.2$, and find weak anticorrelations with the black hole mass and luminosity. Our analysis suggests that the shortest optical variability is driven by a compact region smaller than the accretion disk size, potentially by X-ray reprocessing. Alternatively, this shortest timescale variability suggests that the accretion disk can be inhomogeneous potentially caused by turbulence from magnetorotational instability or magnetic reconnections. (abridged)

AI.tldr: Researchers measured the shortest optical variability timescales in Seyfert 1 galaxies using TESS data, finding upper limits corresponding to light crossing times from compact regions smaller than accretion disk sizes, potentially driven by X-ray reprocessing or disk inhomogeneities.
AI.motivation: To probe the smallest scale structures within AGNs by measuring the shortest detected variability timescales in type 1 radio-quiet Seyfert galaxies, as variability timescales can reveal fundamental physical processes and structures in AGNs.
AI.method: Extracted TESS light curves of 47 Seyfert 1 galaxies, measured their power spectral densities (PSDs) modeled by a power law plus constant noise, and constrained the shortest detected AGN variability timescale as the point where the power law component exceeds the constant noise and systematic uncertainties indicated by upper limits from non-variable quiescent galaxies' PSDs.
AI.result: Measured upper limits of shortest variability timescale: log(t_min,ul/hrs) = 0.85 ± 0.55. These correspond to light crossing scales from a few to thousands of gravitational radii. Found power law index of 2.0 ± 0.2, with weak anticorrelations with black hole mass and luminosity. The measured timescales are smaller than accretion disk sizes measured by other methods.
AI.conclusion: The shortest optical variability in Seyfert 1 galaxies originates from compact regions smaller than accretion disk sizes, potentially driven by X-ray reprocessing or accretion disk inhomogeneities caused by turbulence from magnetorotational instability or magnetic reconnections.

title: Rigorous Formulation of Finite-Sample and Finite-Window Effects in Galaxy Clustering
comment: 8 pages, no figure, submitted
summary: Galaxy surveys provide finite catalogs of objects observed within bounded volumes, yet clustering statistics are often interpreted using theoretical frameworks developed for infinite point processes. In this work, we formulate key statistical quantities directly for finite point processes and examine the structural consequences of finite-number and finite-window constraints. We show that several well-known features of galaxy survey analysis arise naturally from finiteness alone. In particular, non-vanishing higher-order connected correlations can occur even in statistically independent samples when the total number of points is fixed, and the integral constraint in two-point statistics appears as an exact identity implied by the finite-number condition rather than as an estimator artifact. We further demonstrate that counts-in-cells and point-centered environmental measures correspond to distinct statistical ensembles. Using Palm conditioning, we derive an exact relation between random-cell and point-centered statistics, showing that the latter probe a tilted version of the underlying distribution. These results provide a probabilistic framework for separating structural effects imposed by finite sampling from correlations reflecting genuine astrophysical processes. The formulation presented here remains valid for realistic survey geometries and finite data sets and clarifies the interpretation of commonly used clustering statistics in galaxy surveys.

AI.tldr: Finite point process framework for galaxy surveys reveals intrinsic statistical constraints from finite sampling, showing how common clustering features arise from finiteness rather than astrophysics.
AI.motivation: Galaxy surveys analyze finite catalogs within bounded volumes, but clustering statistics often use infinite point process frameworks. This creates a mismatch between theoretical assumptions and observational reality, potentially confusing statistical artifacts with genuine astrophysical correlations.
AI.method: Formulate key statistical quantities directly for finite point processes, examine structural consequences of finite-number and finite-window constraints, use Palm conditioning to derive exact relations between random-cell and point-centered statistics, and analyze counts-in-cells versus point-centered environmental measures.
AI.result: Non-vanishing higher-order connected correlations can occur in statistically independent samples with fixed total points; integral constraint in two-point statistics appears as exact identity from finite-number condition; counts-in-cells and point-centered measures correspond to distinct statistical ensembles; point-centered statistics probe tilted version of underlying distribution.
AI.conclusion: Finite point process framework provides probabilistic foundation for separating structural effects imposed by finite sampling from genuine astrophysical correlations, remaining valid for realistic survey geometries and clarifying interpretation of commonly used clustering statistics.

title: A Long Stellar Stream in M83: Possible Connection Between XUV Disks and Minor Mergers?
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures, Submitted to ApJ
summary: We present the confirmation and characterization of a long stream (S-stream) in the southern part of M83. This feature is revealed using deep wide-field photometric data obtained by the Hyper Suprime-Cam (HSC) mounted on the Subaru Telescope. Using individual red giant branch (RGB) stars, we successfully trace the stream over a large length of $\sim 81$~kpc and a considerable width of $\sim 9$ kpc. With a mean surface brightness of ${\langle μ_{\it V} \rangle} \sim 31.8_{-1.9}^{+1.3}$ mag arcsec$^{-2}$, it is one of the most diffuse extragalactic streams currently known. The mean photometric metallicity of the stream is $\langle[{\rm M/H}]\rangle = -1.23\pm0.02$ dex with a standard deviation of $0.28\pm0.01$ dex, and we estimate the stellar mass to be $(8.5_{-2.8}^{+4.2}) \times 10^6~{\rm M_\odot}$ from the luminosity of RGB stars. Compared to its well-known northern counterpart, the S-stream is slightly more metal-poor, but our large-area RGB map shows compelling evidence that these two features are related, originating from a single low-mass merger event. We identify density variations along the S-stream, which more likely reflect intrinsic density structure within the progenitor rather than the interaction with dark matter subhalos. Similarities between the morphology of the S-stream and some features in the \HI distribution suggest that a minor merger event may have disturbed and redistributed M83's outer \HI gas, leading to triggered star formation and the formation of the XUV disk.

AI.tldr: Discovery and characterization of a long, diffuse stellar stream in M83 using HSC data, tracing it over 81 kpc with metallicity and mass estimates, suggesting it originated from a low-mass merger event.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize faint stellar streams around nearby galaxies to understand their accretion history and the role of minor mergers in galaxy evolution.
AI.method: Used deep wide-field photometric data from Hyper Suprime-Cam on Subaru Telescope to trace individual red giant branch stars, enabling measurement of stream length, width, surface brightness, metallicity, and stellar mass.
AI.result: Discovered an 81 kpc long, 9 kpc wide stream with mean surface brightness μ_V ∼ 31.8 mag arcsec⁻², metallicity [M/H] = -1.23±0.02 dex, and stellar mass of (8.5⁺⁴·²₋₂·₈)×10⁶ M⊙. Density variations suggest intrinsic progenitor structure rather than dark matter subhalo interactions.
AI.conclusion: The S-stream and its northern counterpart originated from a single low-mass merger event, with morphological similarities to HI features suggesting the merger disturbed M83's outer gas, potentially triggering star formation and XUV disk formation.

title: GalSyn I: A Forward-Modeling Framework for Synthetic Galaxy Observations from Hydrodynamical Simulations and First Data Release from IllustrisTNG
comment: 31 pages, 20 figures, submitted to AAS journals. GalSyn is publicly available at https://github.com/aabdurrouf/GalSyn, and its documentation is available at https://galsyn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/. The first public data release is available at https://github.com/aabdurrouf/GalSyn_dr1. Comments are welcome!
summary: We present GalSyn (Galaxy Synthesizer), a modular and flexible Python package for generating synthetic spectrophotometric observations from hydrodynamical galaxy simulations. GalSyn employs a particle-by-particle spectral modeling approach that enables the rapid production of large synthetic datasets required for statistical population studies, offering a computationally efficient alternative to full radiative transfer codes. Users have full control over the spectral modeling choices, including the choice of stellar population synthesis engine, stellar isochrones, spectral libraries, and initial mass functions. Dust attenuation is modeled at the spatially resolved level via a line-of-sight column density method, with a comprehensive suite of fixed and adaptive attenuation laws. A decoupled kinematics model independently Doppler-shifts the stellar and nebular components, enabling realistic synthetic IFU data cubes. It also provides features to add observational realism, including PSF convolution and multi-component noise simulation. Beyond imaging and spectroscopic data cubes, GalSyn reconstructs spatially resolved physical property maps and star formation histories. Alongside this paper, we present the first public data release of synthetic imaging observations and spatially resolved star formation histories generated from the IllustrisTNG simulation suites, comprising four mock extragalactic survey fields (with areas of $5$, $8$, $137$, $365$ arcmin$^{2}$), progenitor histories of 290 local massive galaxies ($\log(M_{*,z=0}/M_{\odot}) > 10.5$) tracked across $0<z<5$, and 259 major-merger systems. Each galaxy data cube contains imaging in 47 filters spanning HST, JWST, Euclid, Rubin/LSST, and the Roman Space Telescope. GalSyn is publicly available at https://github.com/aabdurrouf/GalSyn.

AI.tldr: GalSyn is a Python package for generating synthetic spectrophotometric observations from hydrodynamical galaxy simulations using particle-by-particle spectral modeling, offering computational efficiency over full radiative transfer codes.
AI.motivation: To provide a modular, flexible tool for rapidly producing large synthetic datasets needed for statistical population studies of galaxies, addressing the computational limitations of full radiative transfer codes.
AI.method: Particle-by-particle spectral modeling with user control over stellar population synthesis engines, isochrones, spectral libraries, and IMFs. Includes dust attenuation via line-of-sight column density with various attenuation laws, decoupled kinematics for stellar/nebular components, PSF convolution, and noise simulation.
AI.result: First public data release includes synthetic imaging observations and spatially resolved star formation histories from IllustrisTNG: four mock survey fields (5-365 arcmin²), progenitor histories of 290 massive galaxies tracked across 0<z<5, and 259 major-merger systems with 47-filter imaging spanning multiple telescopes.
AI.conclusion: GalSyn enables efficient generation of realistic synthetic observations for galaxy evolution studies, with public availability and comprehensive data release supporting community research.

title: Imprints of tidal interactions on the stellar distribution of satellite galaxies: implications for dark matter deficient galaxies
comment: 14 pages, 10 figures, 1 table, 3 appendices, submitted to MNRAS
summary: Interactions with the host galaxy strip stars and dark matter from the outer regions of satellite galaxies. Meanwhile, some stars from the central regions can migrate outward due to dynamical heating, producing an excess in the outer surface brightness relative to the extrapolation of the inner Sérsic profile. Recently discovered dark matter deficient galaxies (DMDGs) appear to be representative examples of such tidally disturbed systems. In this work, we investigate how the break radius, defined as the radius beyond which this surface brightness excess emerges, forms and evolves, by performing $N$-body simulations of a satellite galaxy interacting with a host, where the satellite serves as a plausible progenitor of a DMDG. Our simulations naturally reproduce a break radius consistent with that observed in DMDGs. We find that the break radius grows over time and exhibits a characteristic evolutionary behaviour: during each pericentric passage it briefly contracts due to tidal compression, and then rapidly and strongly expands as the satellite undergoes dynamical relaxation. After the satellite reaches a quasi-equilibrium configuration, the break radius shows only mild variations until the next pericentre. Across our suite of simulations, the ratio of the break radius to the effective radius remains approximately constant, even when we change the orbital parameters and internal structure of the satellite. Based on these findings, we develop a prescription for predicting the time evolution of the break radius, which can be used to constrain the tidal interaction history of satellite galaxies, including DMDGs and splashback galaxies.

AI.tldr: N-body simulations show that break radius in tidally disturbed satellite galaxies evolves characteristically: contracts during pericentric passages due to tidal compression, then expands during dynamical relaxation, with break-to-effective radius ratio remaining approximately constant.
AI.motivation: To understand how the break radius (where surface brightness excess emerges in outer regions) forms and evolves in tidally disturbed satellite galaxies, particularly in recently discovered dark matter deficient galaxies (DMDGs) which appear to be representative examples of such systems.
AI.method: Performed N-body simulations of a satellite galaxy interacting with a host galaxy, where the satellite serves as a plausible progenitor of a DMDG, investigating how break radius evolves over time under tidal interactions.
AI.result: Simulations naturally reproduce break radii consistent with observations in DMDGs. Break radius grows over time with characteristic evolution: contracts during pericentric passages due to tidal compression, then rapidly expands during dynamical relaxation. After reaching quasi-equilibrium, break radius shows mild variations until next pericentre. Ratio of break radius to effective radius remains approximately constant across different orbital parameters and satellite internal structures.
AI.conclusion: Developed a prescription for predicting time evolution of break radius that can be used to constrain tidal interaction history of satellite galaxies, including DMDGs and splashback galaxies, based on the characteristic evolutionary behavior observed in simulations.

title: Machine Learning-Based Classification of Active Galaxies and Estimation of Supermassive Black Hole Masses
comment: 9 Pages, 7 Figures, Accepted for publication in Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
summary: Distinguishing active galaxies from star-forming galaxies is essential for understanding galaxy evolution. Diagnostic methods like the BPT (Baldwin, Phillips, and Terlevich) diagram use optical emission-line ratios to separate galaxies. However, with growing availability of large surveys and high-resolution instruments, manually identifying galaxy types has become increasingly challenging. In this study, we investigate machine learning to classify active and star-forming galaxies using properties like stellar mass, stellar velocity dispersion, colour, redshift, and [O III] luminosity. These new approaches enable faster AGN/star-forming galaxy classification than the BPT diagram and provide a flexible, scalable alternative that can complement traditional diagnostics, particularly for large surveys or low-quality data. We employ four classification algorithms -- Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Classifier (SVC), and k-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) -- using the Galaxy Zoo 1 dataset derived from the SDSS sample. The dataset contains 47,675 galaxies within the redshift range 0.02--0.05, including 17,002 pure star-forming and 2,254 active galaxies, labeled using the BPT diagram. These labels train and evaluate our models through confusion matrices, learning curves, and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves. Among the four algorithms, the SVC and Random Forest models achieve the highest accuracy of approximately 93\%, while KNN shows the lowest at 88\%. Furthermore, we estimate supermassive black hole masses using stellar velocity dispersion ($σ$) and the $M_{\rm BH}-σ$ relation. We apply four regression models -- Random Forest Regressor, Support Vector Regressor (SVR), KNN Regressor, and Polynomial Regression. All four models produce similar results, with $R^2$ values from 0.75 to 0.77, indicating consistent performance.

AI.tldr: Machine learning models achieve ~93% accuracy classifying active vs. star-forming galaxies using SDSS data, outperforming manual BPT methods for large surveys.
AI.motivation: Traditional BPT diagram classification becomes impractical for large surveys; need automated, scalable methods to distinguish active galaxies from star-forming galaxies efficiently.
AI.method: Used Galaxy Zoo 1 dataset (47,675 SDSS galaxies, 0.02<z<0.05) with BPT labels. Applied four classification algorithms (Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVC, KNN) using stellar mass, velocity dispersion, color, redshift, and [O III] luminosity features. Also estimated black hole masses via regression models using M_BH-σ relation.
AI.result: SVC and Random Forest achieved highest classification accuracy (~93%), KNN lowest (88%). Regression models for black hole mass estimation showed consistent performance with R² values of 0.75-0.77 across all four methods.
AI.conclusion: Machine learning provides efficient, scalable alternative to BPT diagram for AGN/star-forming galaxy classification, particularly valuable for large surveys and low-quality data where traditional diagnostics are challenging.

title: Massive star clusters detected by JWST as natural birth places to form intermediate-mass black holes
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, submitted to A&A
summary: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) has detected, through gravitational lensing, several young massive star clusters (YMCs), which are considered as relevant building blocks of high redshift galaxies. In this work, we show how a significant fraction of these YMCs could act as relevant birth places for intermediate-mass black holes. We first consider the formation of massive clusters and show that the population of YMCs is consistent with a steep mass-radius relation, which includes a relevant spread of roughly an order of magnitude. We pursue a comparison of this population with young star clusters in the local Universe and Milky Way globular clusters, including an analysis of the characteristic timescales. The YMCs show a wide spread over these properties, but include systems with both short relaxation times as well as relatively short collision timescales, implying they could go through efficient core collapse, which would lead to runaway collisions. We provide quantitative estimates of the sizes of the clusters that could efficiently form intermediate-mass black holes through a runaway collision-based channel, suggesting that these roughly correspond to the systems beyond the $1σ$ scatter in the mass-radius relation. This implies a fraction of ~16% of YMCs as candidates to form intermediate-mass black holes. We show that above a mass limit of ~6x10^6 M_sun, compact star clusters are likely to retain gas even in the presence of strong supernova feedback, altering the dynamics in the central core and providing the possibility to rapidly grow the central object both via gas dynamical friction and Bondi accretion. Finally, we consider the possibility of a gas-dominated regime, in which strong gravitational torques may inhibit star cluster formation and instead directly form a high-mass black holes, as suggested to have occurred in the infinity galaxy.

AI.tldr: JWST-observed young massive clusters could form intermediate-mass black holes via runaway collisions, with ~16% being candidates; gas retention in massive clusters enables additional growth channels.
AI.motivation: JWST has detected young massive star clusters (YMCs) via gravitational lensing, which are important building blocks of high-redshift galaxies. The paper investigates whether these YMCs could serve as birthplaces for intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs).
AI.method: Analyze the population of JWST-detected YMCs, comparing them with local young clusters and Milky Way globular clusters. Examine mass-radius relations, relaxation times, and collision timescales. Identify clusters with short relaxation and collision times that could undergo efficient core collapse leading to runaway collisions. Estimate the fraction of YMCs capable of forming IMBHs. Consider gas retention effects in massive clusters and alternative gas-dominated formation channels.
AI.result: YMCs show a steep mass-radius relation with ~1 order of magnitude spread. Systems beyond the 1σ scatter in this relation (~16% of YMCs) are candidates for efficient IMBH formation via runaway collisions. Clusters above ~6×10⁶ M⊙ can retain gas despite supernova feedback, enabling additional growth via gas dynamical friction and Bondi accretion. Gas-dominated regimes may directly form high-mass black holes.
AI.conclusion: A significant fraction (~16%) of JWST-detected young massive star clusters are viable candidates for intermediate-mass black hole formation through runaway collision channels. Massive clusters can retain gas to enhance central object growth, and gas-dominated environments may enable direct black hole formation without star cluster formation.
